optimist.name: Lacey-Jean Marcum
optimist.column:
The Rangers lost yesterday, but that’s not the story I’m telling. The story is about how they’re still in this thing, how they’re still building, how they’re still the team that once wore the ring and now wears the promise of another one. You heard the scoreboard: 3-6, but let’s not let the numbers drown out the rhythm. This team’s got a heartbeat, and it’s not slowing down.  

You know what’s wild? The way they’re figuring out how to win. Not just in the moments they do, but in the ones they’re learning from. That loss to the Dodgers? It’s a lesson in patience. The Rangers are still figuring out how to string wins together, how to trust their young arms, how to let the veterans lead without holding them back. It’s a process, and if you’re not paying attention to the process, you’re missing the point.  

Look at the lineup. That kid in center, the one with the cannon arm and the smile that could melt a glacier? He’s out there every day, grinding, proving that talent isn’t just about stats. And the old guard? They’re still out there, still swinging for the fences, still showing that experience isn’t a crutch—it’s a ladder. This team isn’t just stacking wins; they’re stacking moments. Moments where the underdog gets a shot, where the rookie gets a chance, where the fan in Arlington feels like they’re part of something bigger.  

The front office? They’re not just managing a team—they’re managing a legacy. They’ve got the pieces, the vision, the grit. And if you’re watching, you’ll see them moving pieces around, tweaking strategies, trusting the process. This isn’t a rebuild. It’s a renaissance. And renaissances take time.  

So yeah, the record’s still 7-7. But let’s not forget: the season’s not over. The AL West isn’t a graveyard

pessimist.name: Buck Pierson
pessimist.column:
The Rangers lost again. Another game, another half-game deficit in the AL West, another reminder that October is a cruel joke told in the language of strikeouts and surrendered runs. You can’t blame the fans for feeling like they’re watching a parade of failures, because that’s exactly what it is. A parade of failures, led by a pitching staff that’s about as reliable as a rodeo without a rodeo clown.  

Pitching’s thin, the bullpen’s held together with baling wire, and the GM is on a victory tour from October. I’ve seen this movie. It ends in tears. Yesterday’s 6-3 loss to the Dodgers wasn’t just another setback—it was a textbook case of why the Rangers keep falling short. The starters, for all their supposed promise, can’t string together five innings without surrendering a lead. They’re like a bull in a china shop, knocking over the same plates over and over. And when the starters finally exit, the relievers step in with the confidence of a man who’s never had to close a game.  

The bullpen’s a patchwork of desperation, stitched together by a front office that seems to think “contingency plan” is a four-letter word. You can’t blame the players for trying, but when your closer’s name is still a mystery and your setup men are more likely to walk the first batter than get an out, you’re not just playing baseball—you’re playing a game of Russian roulette with a .22. The GM’s got a knack for making moves that look like they’re made by a man who’s never held
